questions.question: What are Angular components and how do you create one?
questions.answer:
📌 SIMPLE DEFINITION

An Angular component is the building block of an Angular application — it controls a piece of UI (a view) and its associated logic. Every component has: a TypeScript class (logic), an HTML template (view), and optional CSS styles. Think of it like a portfolio dashboard card: the template is the HTML card layout, the class holds the account data and logic, and CSS styles make it look polished.

✅ ADVANTAGES

AdvantageWhy It Matters
Reusable UI blocksPortfolioCard used on dashboard, watchlist, and profile without duplication
EncapsulationComponent owns its @Input data and @Output events — no internal state leaks
Single ResponsibilityEach component does one thing: PortfolioListComponent handles layout, CardComponent handles detail
Testable in isolationMock @Input/@Output; test logic without backend HTTP calls

🚀 ANGULAR EXAMPLE (TypeScript)

A portfolio list component fetches and displays user portfolios:

@Component({
  selector: 'app-portfolio-list',
  template: `<div *ngFor="let p of portfolios()">{{p.name}}: ₹{{p.value}}</div>`,
  standalone: true,
  imports: [CommonModule]
})
export class PortfolioListComponent implements OnInit {
  portfolios = signal<Portfolio[]>([]);
  constructor(private portfolioService: PortfolioService) {}
  ngOnInit(): void {
    this.portfolioService.getAll()
      .pipe(takeUntilDestroyed())
      .subscribe(data => this.portfolios.set(data));
  }
}

🎤 FINAL INTERVIEW ANSWER

An Angular component is the core building block — a TypeScript class with a template and styles. I use @Component decorator with selector (HTML tag name), templateUrl (HTML file), and standalone: true. Components are reusable: the same PortfolioCard appears on dashboard, search, and watchlist by injecting @Input data. Services are injected via constructor for dependency injection. I use signals for reactive state, takeUntilDestroyed() for cleanup, and *ngIf/*ngFor directives in templates. Each component has one responsibility: PortfolioListComponent shows the list, CardComponent shows a card. They communicate via @Input (parent passes data) and @Output EventEmitter (child sends events). This modular approach scales: add features by adding components, test components in isolation, reuse across the app.

🧠 ONE LINE MEMORY TRICK

Component = TypeScript class + HTML template + CSS styles, reusable and encapsulated.

followUps.question: What is the difference between standalone components and NgModule-based components?
followUps.answer: NgModule-based components (pre-Angular 14) must be declared in an NgModule's declarations array before they can be used. The module manages which components, directives, and pipes are visible to each other and which are exported to other modules. This creates an extra layer of indirection that makes tree-shaking harder and architecture less obvious. Standalone components (Angular 14+, default in Angular 17+) import what they need directly in their @Component decorator via the imports array — no NgModule needed. Simpler: you see all a component's dependencies in one place. Easier lazy loading: standalone components can be lazy-loaded directly. Better tree-shaking: unused imports are clear. NgModules still exist for backward compatibility but all new code should use standalone.

followUps.question: What is a component selector?
followUps.answer: The selector property defines how the component is used in templates. Three formats: element selector ('app-portfolio-list') — used as <app-portfolio-list>, the most common. Attribute selector ('[appHighlight]') — used as <div appHighlight>, typically for directives. Class selector ('.app-portfolio-list') — used as <div class="app-portfolio-list">, rarely used for components. Angular automatically prefixes component selectors with a project prefix (default: app-) defined in angular.json to avoid collisions with standard HTML elements or third-party components. Never use selectors that could conflict with native HTML elements ('button', 'div').

questions.tip: Angular 17+ default is standalone components. NgModules are legacy. New projects should use standalone components. The template: ng new myapp --standalone generates a module-free app.

questions.question: What are Angular lifecycle hooks and when do you use each?
questions.answer:
📌 SIMPLE DEFINITION

Lifecycle hooks are special methods Angular calls at key moments in a component's lifetime: creation (ngOnInit), view rendering (ngAfterViewInit), and destruction (ngOnDestroy). Think of a bank account: created (init), activated (AfterViewInit), and closed (Destroy) — each phase has different logic.

✅ ADVANTAGES

HookRuns WhenUse For
ngOnInitOnce after @Input setLoad data, start subscriptions, initialize charts
ngOnChangesWhen @Input changesReact to parent data changes, recalculate display
ngAfterViewInitAfter DOM renderedAccess @ViewChild elements, initialize Chart.js
ngOnDestroyBefore removalUnsubscribe, cleanup, prevent memory leaks

🚀 ANGULAR EXAMPLE (TypeScript)

Stock price component manages data and cleanup across lifecycle:

@Component({
  selector: 'app-stock-price',
  template: `<p>{{price}}</p><canvas #chart></canvas>`
})
export class StockPriceComponent implements OnInit, AfterViewInit, OnDestroy {
  @Input() symbol!: string;
  @ViewChild('chart') chart!: ElementRef;
  price = 0;

  constructor(private priceService: PriceService) {}

  ngOnInit(): void {
    this.priceService.getPrice(this.symbol)
      .pipe(takeUntilDestroyed())
      .subscribe(p => this.price = p);
  }

  ngAfterViewInit(): void {
    new Chart(this.chart.nativeElement, {...});
  }

  ngOnDestroy(): void {
    console.log(`Cleanup for ${this.symbol}`);
  }
}

🎤 FINAL INTERVIEW ANSWER

Lifecycle hooks execute at specific moments. ngOnInit runs once after @Input properties are set — I fetch portfolio data here. ngOnChanges runs when @Input changes — I recalculate if user selects a different stock. ngAfterViewInit runs after the DOM renders — I can access @ViewChild elements and initialize Chart.js. ngOnDestroy runs before removal — I unsubscribe from Observables to prevent memory leaks. In a live-trading app, forgetting ngOnDestroy causes subscriptions to accumulate, callbacks fire on destroyed components, and memory grows continuously. Angular 16+ offers takeUntilDestroyed() operator to auto-unsubscribe, eliminating manual cleanup. Understanding the order is key: OnInit before AfterViewInit, so I cannot access @ViewChild in ngOnInit.

🧠 ONE LINE MEMORY TRICK

Lifecycle = birth (OnInit), view (AfterViewInit), death (OnDestroy) — clean up in Destroy to prevent leaks.

followUps.question: What happens if you forget to unsubscribe?
followUps.answer: If you subscribe to an Observable and don't unsubscribe in ngOnDestroy, the subscription remains active after the component is destroyed — this is a memory leak. The component instance cannot be garbage collected because the Observable still holds a reference to it. In a long-running SPA where components are created and destroyed frequently (navigation), memory usage grows continuously. Practical impact: event listeners accumulate, callbacks fire on destroyed components (causing "cannot set property of null" errors in the console), and eventually the tab slows down or crashes. HTTP Observables from HttpClient auto-complete after one emission so they don't need unsubscription, but Subject, interval, fromEvent, and route param subscriptions definitely do.

followUps.question: What is the takeUntilDestroyed() operator in Angular 16+?
followUps.answer: takeUntilDestroyed() is an Angular 16+ RxJS operator that automatically completes an Observable when the current component/directive/service is destroyed — no manual Subscription management needed. Call it inside the component (or inject DestroyRef and pass it if calling outside injection context): this.dataService.getData().pipe(takeUntilDestroyed()).subscribe(...). When the component is destroyed, the operator signals completion and the subscription cleans itself up. This replaces the common pattern of private destroy$ = new Subject(); ... takeUntil(this.destroy$) ... ngOnDestroy() { this.destroy$.next(); }. Cleaner, less boilerplate, and less error-prone — it's the recommended way to manage subscriptions in Angular 16+ applications.

questions.tip: takeUntilDestroyed() (Angular 16+) is the cleanest way to auto-unsubscribe: this.dataService.getChartData().pipe(takeUntilDestroyed()).subscribe(...). No manual Subscription management needed.

questions.question: What is RxJS and how do you use it in Angular?
questions.answer:
📌 SIMPLE DEFINITION

RxJS (Reactive Extensions JavaScript) is a library for handling asynchronous data streams with Observables — values that arrive over time (API responses, user clicks, WebSocket messages). Think of a live price ticker: prices stream in continuously, you filter (only gainers), transform (format as rupees), and react to changes.

✅ ADVANTAGES

AdvantageWhy It Matters
Compose async cleanlyNo callback hell — pipe() operators transform streams elegantly
Transform in-flightmap(), filter(), debounceTime() transform price stream before display
Cancellableunsubscribe() stops API calls, WebSocket listeners on demand
Angular integratedHttpClient, form events, route changes all return Observables

🚀 ANGULAR EXAMPLE (TypeScript)

Portfolio dashboard streams stock prices and filters gainers in real-time:

export class PriceSearchComponent implements OnInit {
  searchControl = new FormControl();
  displayedStocks = signal<Stock[]>([]);

  constructor(private priceService: PriceService) {}

  ngOnInit(): void {
    this.searchControl.valueChanges
      .pipe(
        debounceTime(300),        // Wait 300ms after typing stops
        distinctUntilChanged(),    // Only if value changed
        switchMap(q => this.priceService.search(q)),  // Cancel old, start new
        takeUntilDestroyed()
      )
      .subscribe(stocks => this.displayedStocks.set(stocks));
  }
}

📊 DIFFERENCE TABLE

AspectPromiseObservable
EmissionsOne valueMany values over time
LazyExecutes immediatelyOnly on subscribe
CancellableNoYes — unsubscribe()
OperatorsLimited100+ (map, filter, switchMap, debounce)

🎤 FINAL INTERVIEW ANSWER

RxJS is a library for streams of asynchronous data. In Angular, HTTP calls return Observables, so I pipe() them through operators: map() transforms, filter() selects, debounceTime() delays to avoid hammering the API, switchMap() cancels old requests when the user types new text. In FolioTrack's search, I debounce user input 300ms, filter unchanged values, and switchMap to a new API search — this prevents sending a request on every keystroke. I subscribe() to get values and unsubscribe() to stop. Promises can only emit one value and execute immediately; Observables emit many values and are lazy. For real-time trading data or WebSocket streams, Observables are essential — I can merge multiple price feeds, filter by performance, and auto-unsubscribe when the component destroys. takeUntilDestroyed() operator in Angular 16+ auto-unsubscribes, eliminating manual cleanup.

🧠 ONE LINE MEMORY TRICK

Observable = stream of async data; pipe() to transform it; subscribe() to listen; unsubscribe() to stop.

followUps.question: What is the difference between switchMap, mergeMap, and concatMap?
followUps.answer: All three map inner Observables but handle concurrency differently: switchMap — cancels the previous inner Observable when a new one arrives. Use for HTTP requests where only the latest result matters (search autocomplete, live filtering). If the user types quickly, only the last query's request completes. mergeMap (flatMap) — subscribes to all inner Observables concurrently. Use for parallel operations (uploading multiple files simultaneously). All results come back, in any order. concatMap — queues inner Observables and processes them one at a time in order. Use for sequential operations where order matters (saving items one by one). The wrong choice causes bugs: using mergeMap for search returns stale results; using concatMap for search creates a backlog of queued requests.

followUps.question: What is a BehaviorSubject?
followUps.answer: A BehaviorSubject is a Subject that holds and emits the current value to new subscribers — unlike a regular Subject which only emits future values. New subscribers immediately get the last emitted value. Use it for state that components need the current value of immediately upon subscription: current user, theme preference, cart contents. Example: private currentUser$ = new BehaviorSubject<User | null>(null). Components subscribe and immediately get null (or the logged-in user if already authenticated). When the user logs in, this.currentUser$.next(user) emits to all current and future subscribers. Expose it as readonly currentUser$ = this.currentUser$.asObservable() to prevent external code from calling next() directly.

questions.tip: switchMap vs mergeMap vs concatMap: switchMap = cancel previous (HTTP search). mergeMap = run all concurrently (parallel uploads). concatMap = queue them, run one at a time (sequential operations). Wrong choice causes duplicate requests or missed responses.

questions.question: What is Angular's change detection and how does OnPush work?
questions.answer:
📌 SIMPLE DEFINITION

Angular's change detection finds what changed and re-renders. Default checks everything after every event. OnPush only checks when @Input changes or events fire — much faster for large UIs. Think of a stock ticker: OnPush rerenders only when the price data object reference changes, not every millisecond.

✅ ADVANTAGES

StrategySpeedConstraint
DefaultSlower (checks all)None — safe, catches all changes
OnPush2-5x fasterUse immutable @Input, markForCheck() for service updates

🚀 ANGULAR EXAMPLE (TypeScript)

Portfolio card with OnPush only updates when data reference changes:

@Component({
  selector: 'app-portfolio-card',
  template: `<h3>{{portfolio.name}}</h3>`,
  changeDetection: ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush
})
export class PortfolioCardComponent {
  @Input() portfolio!: Portfolio;  // Change triggers re-render
  @Output() buy = new EventEmitter();

  selectPortfolio(): void {
    this.buy.emit(this.portfolio);
  }
}

// Parent must pass immutable new object
this.portfolios.set([...this.portfolios(), newPortfolio]);  // Creates new array reference
// NOT this.portfolios()[0].value = 100;  // Mutates existing — OnPush won't detect

🎤 FINAL INTERVIEW ANSWER

Change detection is Angular's process of finding what changed and re-rendering. Default checks all components after every event — safe but slow on large apps with hundreds of cards. OnPush only checks when @Input properties change or events fire in that component — 3-5x faster. In FolioTrack with 500+ portfolio cards, OnPush is essential. The constraint: you must use immutable patterns — when updating @Input data, assign a new object reference (spread operator), not mutate the existing one. For service updates outside @Input, call markForCheck(). Angular 17 signals work seamlessly with OnPush: when signal() changes, change detection runs automatically. Rule: use OnPush everywhere in production; Default is only for small demos. OnPush separates concerns: parent owns state changes, child responds to clean inputs.

🧠 ONE LINE MEMORY TRICK

Default = check everything always (slow). OnPush = check only when @Input changes or events fire (fast).

followUps.question: What is Zone.js?
followUps.answer: Zone.js is Angular's mechanism for knowing when to run change detection. It monkey-patches all async browser APIs (setTimeout, Promises, XHR, event listeners) and notifies Angular after every async operation completes. Angular then runs change detection to check if the UI needs updating. Zone.js is what makes Angular "just work" — you update a property in a setTimeout callback and the view updates automatically. The downside: Zone.js can't distinguish between operations that affect the UI and those that don't, causing unnecessary change detection cycles. Angular 17 introduced Signals which provide fine-grained reactivity without Zone.js. Angular 18+ allows running applications in zoneless mode (experimental) for maximum performance.

followUps.question: What are Angular Signals (Angular 17+)?
followUps.answer: Angular Signals are a reactive primitive for fine-grained state management. A signal is a wrapper around a value that notifies consumers when it changes. const count = signal(0) creates a signal. count() reads it. count.set(1) or count.update(v => v + 1) changes it. computed(() => count() * 2) creates a derived signal that only recomputes when inputs change. effect(() => console.log(count())) runs a side effect whenever the signal changes. In templates, signal-based components know exactly which signals they read — Angular can re-render only the parts of the template that depend on changed signals, not the entire component tree. FolioTrack (Angular 19) uses signals throughout for state management instead of BehaviorSubject + async pipe.

questions.tip: Angular Signals (v17+) provide fine-grained reactivity without Zone.js. A signal() knows exactly which components read it and only re-renders those. Much more efficient than the dirty-checking approach. FolioTrack (Angular 19) uses signals throughout.

questions.question: What is the difference between Reactive Forms and Template-driven Forms in Angular?
questions.answer:
📌 SIMPLE DEFINITION

Reactive Forms use TypeScript FormControl and FormGroup objects to manage form state and validation. Template-driven Forms use ngModel in templates. Reactive is explicit and testable; Template-driven is quick but less flexible for complex forms.

✅ ADVANTAGES

AspectReactiveTemplate-driven
Model locationCode (FormGroup)Template (ngModel)
ValidationCode + custom validatorsDirectives, limited
Dynamic fieldsFormArray (easy)Complex ngIf loops
TestingUnit test without renderingRender template to test

🚀 ANGULAR EXAMPLE (TypeScript)

KYC form using Reactive Forms with custom PAN validation:

@Component({
  selector: 'app-kyc-form',
  template: `
    <form [formGroup]="kycForm" (ngSubmit)="submit()">
      <input formControlName="fullName" placeholder="Full Name">
      <input formControlName="pan" placeholder="PAN">
      <button [disabled]="kycForm.invalid">Submit</button>
    </form>
  `,
  standalone: true,
  imports: [ReactiveFormsModule]
})
export class KycFormComponent {
  kycForm = this.fb.group({
    fullName: ['', [Validators.required, Validators.minLength(5)]],
    pan: ['', [Validators.required, this.panValidator]]
  });

  constructor(private fb: FormBuilder) {}

  panValidator(control: AbstractControl) {
    return /^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]{1}$/.test(control.value) ? null : { invalidPan: true };
  }

  submit(): void {
    if (this.kycForm.valid) {
      console.log(this.kycForm.value);
    }
  }
}

🎤 FINAL INTERVIEW ANSWER

Reactive Forms give you an explicit form model in TypeScript using FormBuilder and FormGroup. Each field is a FormControl with validators. I build the form in ngOnInit, define custom validators (PAN format, password match), and bind with formControlName. The form state is explicit and testable without rendering. Template-driven Forms are simpler for quick, small forms: use ngModel and template validators. For complex forms like KYC, investment questionnaires, or multi-step workflows, Reactive Forms win. I can dynamically add/remove fields with FormArray, access form state programmatically, and test validation logic without rendering. In FolioTrack's onboarding, Reactive Forms handle 50+ fields across 5 steps with cross-field validation and progressive disclosure.

🧠 ONE LINE MEMORY TRICK

Reactive = form in TypeScript (explicit, powerful). Template-driven = form in HTML (quick, limited).

followUps.question: What is FormArray?
followUps.answer: FormArray manages a list of dynamic form controls that can grow or shrink at runtime — like a table of rows the user can add or remove. Create it with this.fb.array([]). Add rows: this.holdings.push(this.fb.group({ symbol: [''], quantity: [0] })). Remove rows: this.holdings.removeAt(index). In the template, iterate with *ngFor="let control of holdings.controls; let i = index" and use [formGroupName]="i". The entire array is validated — if any row is invalid, the array is invalid. In FolioTrack's portfolio creation, users could add multiple stock holdings in one form — FormArray made this natural to implement and validate.

followUps.question: How do you add custom validators to reactive forms?
followUps.answer: A custom validator is a function that takes an AbstractControl and returns either null (valid) or a ValidationErrors object (invalid). Synchronous: const noSpaces = (ctrl: AbstractControl) => ctrl.value?.includes(' ') ? { noSpaces: true } : null. Async validator: returns Observable<ValidationErrors | null> — useful for checking email uniqueness in the DB. Apply to a control: fb.control('', [Validators.required, noSpaces], [emailUniqueValidator]). Cross-field validator: apply to a group and access multiple controls via group.get('password') and group.get('confirmPassword'). Display in template: *ngIf="form.get('name').errors?.noSpaces".

questions.tip: Reactive forms for anything non-trivial. Template-driven forms become unmanageable when you need conditional validation, dynamic rows, or async validators (check email uniqueness). Reactive forms keep all that logic in testable TypeScript.

questions.question: What is data binding in Angular and what are its four types?
questions.answer:
📌 SIMPLE DEFINITION

Directives extend HTML with Angular logic. Structural directives change DOM structure (*ngIf removes/adds elements, *ngFor repeats). Attribute directives change appearance ([ngClass] applies CSS, [ngStyle] sets styles). Components are directives with templates.

✅ ADVANTAGES

TypePurposeExamples
StructuralAdd/remove DOM elements*ngIf, *ngFor, *ngSwitch
AttributeChange appearance/behavior[ngClass], [ngStyle], custom
ComponentFull UI block with templatePortfolioCard, Header, Footer

🚀 ANGULAR EXAMPLE (TypeScript)

Portfolio list with structural and attribute directives:

@Component({
  template: `
    <!-- *ngIf: Structural -->
    <p *ngIf="portfolios.length === 0">No data</p>
    
    <!-- *ngFor: Structural -->
    <div *ngFor="let p of portfolios" [ngClass]="{ 'gain': p.profit > 0 }"
         [ngStyle]="{ color: p.profit > 0 ? 'green' : 'red' }">
      {{ p.name }}: {{ p.profit }}
    </div>
  `,
  standalone: true,
  imports: [CommonModule]
})
export class PortfolioListComponent {
  portfolios = [
    { name: 'Growth', profit: 15000 },
    { name: 'Safe', profit: -2000 }
  ];
}

🎤 FINAL INTERVIEW ANSWER

Structural directives like *ngIf and *ngFor change the DOM structure. *ngIf="portfolios.length === 0" removes the element if false, adds it if true. *ngFor="let p of portfolios" repeats the element for each item. Attribute directives like [ngClass] and [ngStyle] don't change DOM, just appearance. [ngClass]="{ 'gainers': p.profit > 0 }" conditionally applies CSS classes; [ngStyle]="{ color: p.profit > 0 ? 'green' : 'red' }" sets inline styles. I can create custom directives: an appHighlight directive sets background color based on data. Components are specialized directives with templates and logic. In FolioTrack's dashboard, *ngIf controls visibility of empty states, *ngFor repeats cards for each portfolio, and [ngClass] highlights top performers green and losers red.

🧠 ONE LINE MEMORY TRICK

Structural = *change DOM. Attribute = [change appearance]. Component = .

followUps.question: What is the difference between property binding and attribute binding?
followUps.answer: Property binding sets a DOM property — a runtime JavaScript property on the element object. Attribute binding sets an HTML attribute — the value written in the HTML markup. Most of the time they mirror each other, but not always. Example: [colspan]="2" fails because there is no colspan DOM property — you must use [attr.colspan]="2" to set the HTML attribute. Another example: value attribute (initial value in HTML) vs value property (current runtime value) — after a user types in an input, the attribute is unchanged but the property reflects what was typed. Rule of thumb: use property binding by default. Use [attr.X] when the binding target is a pure HTML attribute with no matching DOM property (typically ARIA attributes like [attr.aria-label] and table attributes like [attr.colspan]).

followUps.question: Can you use two-way binding without ngModel?
followUps.answer: Yes — the [(x)] syntax is just syntactic sugar. It expands to [x]="value" (xChange)="value = $event". Any component can implement two-way binding by creating an @Input() x and a corresponding @Output() xChange = new EventEmitter(). When the child calls this.xChange.emit(newValue), the parent's binding updates automatically. This is the same contract ngModel uses internally. Angular 17.1 introduced model inputs (model()) as the signal-based replacement — cleaner than the @Input/@Output pair: value = model(0) creates both an input and an output signal automatically.

questions.tip: Mnemonic for syntax: [] = data flows in (to DOM). () = events flow out (from DOM). [()] = banana in a box = both directions. Square brackets go on left (like assignment LHS), parens go on right (like function calls).

questions.question: What are Angular directives and what are the different types?
questions.answer:
📌 SIMPLE DEFINITION

Decorators are functions prefixed with @ that add metadata to classes and properties. @Component makes a class a component; @Input marks a property as receivable from parent; @Output marks a property that sends events to parent; @Injectable marks a service.

✅ ADVANTAGES

DecoratorWhat It DoesUse Case
@ComponentMark class as componentEvery UI component
@InputReceive data from parentChild component properties
@OutputSend events to parentChild emits on user actions
@InjectableMark as injectable serviceServices for DI

🚀 ANGULAR EXAMPLE (TypeScript)

Parent-child with decorators:

@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })
export class PortfolioService { ... }

@Component({
  selector: 'app-portfolio-card',
  template: `<button (click)="buy()">Buy</button>`
})
export class PortfolioCardComponent {
  @Input() portfolio!: Portfolio;        // Receive from parent
  @Output() buyRequested = new EventEmitter();

  buy(): void {
    this.buyRequested.emit(this.portfolio.id);  // Send to parent
  }
}

@Component({
  template: `<app-portfolio-card [portfolio]="p" (buyRequested)="onBuy($event)">`
})
export class DashboardComponent {
  constructor(private portfolioService: PortfolioService) {}  // DI
}

🎤 FINAL INTERVIEW ANSWER

Decorators add metadata to code. @Component decorates a class as an Angular component with selector, templateUrl, and standalone flag. @Input marks a property that the parent passes data to — [portfolio]="p" binds parent data to the child's portfolio property. @Output marks an EventEmitter that the child uses to send events to parent — (buyRequested)="onBuy($event)" listens to the child's event. @Injectable marks a class as a service that Angular's DI can inject — providedIn: 'root' makes it a singleton. In FolioTrack, PortfolioCardComponent receives a portfolio via @Input and emits buyRequested via @Output. DashboardComponent passes portfolios to cards and listens for events. PortfolioService has @Injectable so every component can inject it via constructor.

🧠 ONE LINE MEMORY TRICK

@Component = component. @Input/@Output = parent-child messaging. @Injectable = can be injected.

followUps.question: What is trackBy in *ngFor and why is it important?
followUps.answer: By default, when the array passed to *ngFor changes, Angular re-renders every DOM element in the list — even items that didn't change. trackBy tells Angular which field uniquely identifies each item so it can re-use existing DOM elements and only update the ones that actually changed. Without trackBy on a list of 100 items, replacing the array destroys and recreates 100 DOM nodes. With trackBy: trackById, Angular keeps unchanged nodes and only updates the diff. This is critical for performance: reduced DOM manipulation, animations don't flash, and input focus is preserved. Implementation: trackById(index: number, item: Portfolio) { return item.id; }. In FolioTrack's holdings table with real-time NAV updates, trackBy prevented the entire table from flickering every second.

followUps.question: What is the difference between *ngIf and [hidden]?
followUps.answer: *ngIf removes the element and all its children from the DOM entirely when the condition is false — the component is destroyed, lifecycle hooks run, subscriptions are cleaned up. [hidden] only sets display: none — the element stays in the DOM, the component remains alive, and all its subscriptions and memory usage persist. Use *ngIf for conditional rendering that genuinely shouldn't exist (auth-gated content, empty states) — it's cleaner and releases memory. Use [hidden] only when the toggle happens very frequently and the recreation cost is too high (e.g., a tab panel where switching tabs shouldn't destroy form state). Angular 17's new @if / @for / @switch control flow blocks replace the ngIf/ngFor/ngSwitch directives and are now the recommended approach.

questions.tip: Angular 17+ prefers the new control flow syntax (@if, @for, @switch) over *ngIf/*ngFor/*ngSwitch. Always add trackBy (or track in the new @for syntax) to any *ngFor/@for that renders more than a handful of items.

questions.question: What are Angular decorators? Explain @Component, @Input, @Output, and @Injectable.
questions.answer:
📌 SIMPLE DEFINITION

Parent-to-child communication passes data via @Input properties. Child-to-parent uses @Output EventEmitter to send events back. Parent owns data, passes it down; child reacts and sends events up.

✅ ADVANTAGES

DirectionMechanismExample
Parent → Child@Input + property binding[portfolio]="portfolioData"
Child → Parent@Output + event binding(buyClicked)="handleBuy($event)"

🚀 ANGULAR EXAMPLE (TypeScript)

Dashboard passes portfolio to card, card emits buy event:

// Child: PortfolioCardComponent
@Component({
  selector: 'app-portfolio-card',
  template: `
    <h3>{{ portfolio.name }}</h3>
    <button (click)="onBuy()">Buy</button>
  `
})
export class PortfolioCardComponent {
  @Input() portfolio!: Portfolio;
  @Output() buyClicked = new EventEmitter<string>();

  onBuy(): void {
    this.buyClicked.emit(this.portfolio.id);
  }
}

// Parent: DashboardComponent
@Component({
  template: `
    <app-portfolio-card
      *ngFor="let p of portfolios"
      [portfolio]="p"
      (buyClicked)="handleBuy($event)">
    </app-portfolio-card>
  `
})
export class DashboardComponent {
  portfolios = [{id: 'p1', name: 'Growth'}];

  handleBuy(portfolioId: string): void {
    console.log(`Buy: ${portfolioId}`);
  }
}

🎤 FINAL INTERVIEW ANSWER

Parent-to-child passes data using @Input: the parent binds [portfolio]="portfolioData", and the child receives it via @Input property. Child-to-parent uses @Output EventEmitter: the child emits events via emit(), the parent listens with (buyClicked)="handler($event)". This unidirectional flow — data down, events up — decouples components. In FolioTrack, DashboardComponent passes each portfolio to PortfolioCardComponent via @Input, and each card independently emits buyClicked events that the dashboard handles without tight coupling. For sibling communication or complex state, use a shared service with Subject. This pattern scales: reuse the card on multiple pages, change parent data, and all cards update automatically.

🧠 ONE LINE MEMORY TRICK

Parent → Child = [data]. Child → Parent = (event). Data down, events up.

followUps.question: What is the difference between providedIn: 'root' and providing in a component?
followUps.answer: providedIn: 'root' registers the service in the root injector — one singleton instance shared across the entire app. This is the correct choice for most services (HTTP services, auth, state). providedIn: 'root' also enables tree-shaking: if the service is never injected, it's excluded from the bundle. Providing in a component (@Component({ providers: [MyService] })) creates a new instance for that component and all its descendants — the instance is destroyed when the component is destroyed. Use this when you need component-scoped state: a wizard's step service, a popover's data service. Providing in a feature module (lazy-loaded) gives you module-scoped instances — once the module loads, all components in it share the same instance.

followUps.question: What is @NgModule decorator?
followUps.answer: @NgModule defines a cohesive block of functionality in the traditional Angular architecture (pre-standalone). It has four key arrays: declarations — components, directives, and pipes that belong to this module; imports — other modules whose exported declarations this module needs; exports — declarations this module shares with modules that import it; providers — services registered with this module's injector (deprecated in favour of providedIn); bootstrap — the root component (only in AppModule). In Angular 17+ with standalone components, @NgModule is optional — components manage their own dependencies via the imports array in @Component. But you'll still encounter NgModules in existing codebases and third-party libraries.

questions.tip: Angular 16+ introduced @Input({ required: true }) for required inputs — Angular will throw a compile-time error if a parent doesn't provide it. Much safer than runtime undefined checks. Also new: @Input({ transform: booleanAttribute }) for attribute coercion.

questions.question: How do you implement parent-to-child and child-to-parent communication in Angular?
questions.answer:
📌 SIMPLE DEFINITION

Sibling components (not parent-child) communicate via a shared service with RxJS Subject or BehaviorSubject. One sibling emits data through the service; another subscribes and reacts. Like message brokers, not direct calls.

✅ ADVANTAGES

PatternWhen to UseExample
SubjectSiblings emit discrete eventsTradePanel listens to PortfolioPanel selection
BehaviorSubjectNeed current value for late subscribersCurrentUserService — always has logged-in user

🚀 ANGULAR EXAMPLE (TypeScript)

Shared service with Subject for sibling communication:

// Shared service
@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })
export class SelectionService {
  private portfolioSelected = new Subject<Portfolio>();
  portfolioSelected$ = this.portfolioSelected.asObservable();

  selectPortfolio(portfolio: Portfolio): void {
    this.portfolioSelected.next(portfolio);
  }
}

// Sibling 1: PortfolioListComponent
@Component({
  template: `
    <button *ngFor="let p of portfolios" (click)="select(p)">
      {{ p.name }}
    </button>
  `
})
export class PortfolioListComponent {
  portfolios = [{id: 'p1', name: 'Growth'}];
  constructor(private selection: SelectionService) {}

  select(portfolio: Portfolio): void {
    this.selection.selectPortfolio(portfolio);
  }
}

// Sibling 2: TradeFormComponent
@Component({
  template: `<p *ngIf="selectedPortfolio">Trade: {{ selectedPortfolio.name }}</p>`
})
export class TradeFormComponent implements OnInit {
  selectedPortfolio: Portfolio | null = null;
  constructor(private selection: SelectionService) {}

  ngOnInit(): void {
    this.selection.portfolioSelected$
      .pipe(takeUntilDestroyed())
      .subscribe(p => this.selectedPortfolio = p);
  }
}

🎤 FINAL INTERVIEW ANSWER

Siblings don't have direct parent-child access, so they communicate via a shared service. SelectionService uses Subject to broadcast events: when PortfolioListComponent selects a portfolio, it calls selectPortfolio(), which emits via the Subject. TradeFormComponent subscribes to portfolioSelected$ and reacts without coupling to the list. BehaviorSubject is similar but also provides the current value to late subscribers — CurrentUserService always emits the logged-in user, so any new component can subscribe and get the current user instantly. For complex app state (FolioTrack's holdings, balance, transactions), use NgRx or Akita — they scale better than raw Subjects. The pattern is powerful: siblings remain decoupled, each owns their view, and services act as state brokers.

🧠 ONE LINE MEMORY TRICK

Sibling communication = shared service with Subject (event broker). No direct coupling.

followUps.question: What is the difference between @ViewChild and @ContentChild?
followUps.answer: @ViewChild queries elements in the component's own template (its view). @ContentChild queries elements projected into the component via <ng-content> (its content). Example: a card component that wraps projected content. The wrapper's template is its view (@ViewChild); what users place inside <app-card>...</app-card> is its content (@ContentChild). Timing: @ViewChild is available in ngAfterViewInit; @ContentChild is available in ngAfterContentInit. Both are undefined before their respective lifecycle hooks fire. Use { static: true } in the decorator when you need the reference in ngOnInit (only works if the element isn't inside a structural directive like *ngIf).

followUps.question: What are Angular Signals model inputs (Angular 17.1+)?
followUps.answer: Angular 17.1 introduced model() as the signal-based replacement for the @Input() + @Output(xChange) two-way binding pair. Declare: value = model(0). This creates a signal that acts as both an input (parent can bind to it) and an output (when the component calls this.value.set(n), the parent's binding updates). In the parent: [(value)]="parentValue" or [value]="parentValue" (valueChange)="parentValue = $event". Benefits: less boilerplate than @Input+@Output, fine-grained change detection (no Zone.js), and better TypeScript inference. It's the modern way to implement two-way binding on custom components in Angular 17.1+.

questions.tip: The rule: data flows down via @Input (parent → child), events flow up via @Output (child → parent). Never reach up from a child to modify its parent directly — always emit an event. This keeps data flow predictable and components testable.

questions.question: How do sibling components communicate in Angular?
questions.answer:
📌 SIMPLE DEFINITION

Pipes transform data for display in templates. Built-in pipes like date, currency format strings and numbers. Custom pipes extend functionality for domain-specific needs. Data goes in, transformed data comes out.

✅ ADVANTAGES

TypePurposeExample
Built-inFormat dates, numbers, currency, text{{ date | date: 'short' }} → 1/15/24
CustomDomain-specific transforms{{ amount | inrCurrency }} → ₹1,00,000
ChainedMultiple transforms{{ name | uppercase | slice:0:3 }}

🚀 ANGULAR EXAMPLE (TypeScript)

Custom currency pipe for fintech:

@Pipe({
  name: 'inrCurrency',
  standalone: true
})
export class InrCurrencyPipe implements PipeTransform {
  transform(value: number): string {
    if (!value) return '₹0';
    return new Intl.NumberFormat('en-IN', {
      style: 'currency',
      currency: 'INR',
      minimumFractionDigits: 2
    }).format(value);
  }
}

// Usage
@Component({
  template: `
    <!-- Built-in pipes -->
    <p>{{ createdDate | date: 'medium' }}</p>
    <p>{{ portfolio | currency: 'INR' }}</p>

    <!-- Custom pipe -->
    <p>Value: {{ value | inrCurrency }}</p>
    
    <!-- Chained pipes -->
    <p>{{ name | uppercase | slice:0:3 }}</p>
  `
})
export class PortfolioComponent {
  value = 1500000;
}

🎤 FINAL INTERVIEW ANSWER

Pipes transform data in templates. Angular's built-in pipes like date, currency, uppercase, and slice format strings and numbers. For fintech, custom pipes are essential: InrCurrencyPipe formats 1500000 as ₹15,00,000 in Indian style. I implement PipeTransform.transform() and decorate with @Pipe. Pipes are chainable: {{ name | uppercase | slice:0:3 }} uppercases then takes first 3 characters. They're pure functions — same input always gives same output — so Angular caches results for performance. Use pipes for presentation logic only, not side effects or expensive operations. In FolioTrack, I use currency pipes on prices, date pipes on transaction timestamps, and custom PercentageChangePipe to add +/- signs and colors to returns.

🧠 ONE LINE MEMORY TRICK

Pipe = data transformer in templates: {{ value | pipe: param }}. Built-in for common formats, custom for domain logic.

followUps.question: When would you use Subject vs BehaviorSubject?
followUps.answer: Use BehaviorSubject when the state has a current value that new subscribers need immediately — selected item, current user, theme, cart contents. New subscribers get the latest emitted value right away without waiting for the next emission. Use Subject (no initial value, no replay) when you're emitting events rather than state — "a button was clicked", "a file was uploaded". New subscribers only receive events that happen after they subscribe. Use ReplaySubject(n) when late subscribers need the last N emissions — useful for cache-like behavior or when components might subscribe after initial data loads. Use AsyncSubject when only the final value matters (emits only on complete) — rarely used in Angular apps.

followUps.question: What is the alternative to a shared service for sibling communication?
followUps.answer: The cleanest alternative is to lift state up to the common parent and use @Input/@Output — the parent holds the selected portfolio, passes it down to the detail component via @Input, and receives selection events from the list via @Output. This keeps all state management visible in one place and makes the siblings stateless/dumb components. Use a shared service when: the siblings are far apart in the component tree (many levels up to common parent), when many unrelated components need the same state, or when the state needs to persist across route changes. For larger apps, consider NgRx (Redux pattern), Akita, or Angular's Signals-based state (signal stores) to manage complex cross-component state in a predictable, debuggable way.

questions.tip: Rule of thumb: Start with lifting state to the parent + @Input/@Output. Switch to a shared service when the component tree gets too deep or too many components need the same state. Avoid EventBus/global events — they create invisible couplings that are hard to trace.

questions.question: What are Angular pipes and how do you create a custom pipe?
questions.answer:
📌 SIMPLE DEFINITION

HTTP Interceptors intercept and modify every HTTP request/response globally before they leave or after they return. Like customs at an airport: every HTTP request gets checked (add auth headers) before boarding.

✅ ADVANTAGES

Use CaseBenefitExample
AuthAttach JWT token to every requestAuthorization header on all API calls
Error handlingCatch 401, refresh token, retryAuto-refresh and retry on token expiry
LoadingShow spinner on any HTTP activityLoadingService increment/decrement

🚀 ANGULAR EXAMPLE (TypeScript)

Auth interceptor adds JWT and handles 401:

@Injectable()
export class AuthInterceptor implements HttpInterceptor {
  constructor(private auth: AuthService) {}

  intercept(req: HttpRequest<any>, next: HttpHandler): Observable<HttpEvent<any>> {
    // Add JWT token
    const token = this.auth.getToken();
    if (token) {
      req = req.clone({
        setHeaders: { Authorization: `Bearer ${token}` }
      });
    }

    return next.handle(req).pipe(
      catchError((err: HttpErrorResponse) => {
        if (err.status === 401) {
          // Token expired — refresh and retry
          return this.auth.refreshToken().pipe(
            switchMap(newToken => {
              const newReq = req.clone({
                setHeaders: { Authorization: `Bearer ${newToken}` }
              });
              return next.handle(newReq);
            })
          );
        }
        return throwError(() => err);
      })
    );
  }
}

// Register in app.config.ts
providers: [{ provide: HTTP_INTERCEPTORS, useClass: AuthInterceptor, multi: true }]

🎤 FINAL INTERVIEW ANSWER

HTTP Interceptors are middleware that intercept every HTTP request and response. They implement HttpInterceptor with an intercept() method. In FolioTrack, AuthInterceptor adds the JWT token to every request's Authorization header — components don't repeat boilerplate. It also handles 401 responses: if the token expires, the interceptor calls refreshToken(), updates the token, and retries the original request transparently. This is cleaner than error handling in every component. I also use a LoadingInterceptor to track active requests and show a spinner. Interceptors chain: request goes through each, response comes back through each in reverse. This centralized approach scales: add logging, caching, or retry logic once, and all API calls benefit. Multiple interceptors can be registered with HTTP_INTERCEPTORS and multi: true.

🧠 ONE LINE MEMORY TRICK

Interceptor = middleware customs checkpoint for every HTTP request — add auth, handle errors, log globally.

followUps.question: What is the difference between pure and impure pipes?
followUps.answer: Pure pipes (default) are only re-evaluated when the input value reference changes — same as OnPush change detection. Angular caches the result and reuses it if the input hasn't changed. Fast and efficient. Impure pipes (pure: false) are re-evaluated on every change detection cycle, regardless of input changes. Use only when the pipe must react to changes deep inside an object or array (e.g., filtering a mutable array). Impure pipes can severely impact performance — they run hundreds of times per second in busy UIs. Prefer immutable data + pure pipes: instead of mutating an array and using an impure filter pipe, create a new filtered array in the component and bind to that. The async pipe is impure (it must react to each Observable emission). JsonPipe and SlicePipe are also impure.

followUps.question: What is the AsyncPipe and why is it preferred over manual subscription?
followUps.answer: The AsyncPipe subscribes to an Observable or Promise in the template and returns the latest emitted value. When the component is destroyed, it automatically unsubscribes — no manual ngOnDestroy needed. Usage: {{ data$ | async }} or *ngIf="data$ | async as data". Benefits: no memory leaks (auto-unsubscribes), works perfectly with OnPush change detection (it calls markForCheck() on each emission), and less boilerplate code. The pattern *ngIf="vm$ | async as vm" (view model observable) lets you use a single subscription for all component data and avoids nested async pipes. In FolioTrack's dashboard, I used combineLatest([holdings$, prices$]).pipe(map(([h,p]) => computeViewModel(h,p))) as a single vm$ observable piped through async.

questions.tip: Always use pure pipes. If you find yourself needing an impure pipe, the real fix is usually to make your data immutable (return a new array instead of mutating the existing one). The async pipe is the safest way to consume Observables in templates — it handles subscription and cleanup automatically.

questions.question: What are HTTP Interceptors in Angular and how do you create one?
questions.answer:
📌 SIMPLE DEFINITION

NgModule organizes related components, directives, pipes, and services into cohesive blocks. Declarations list what's inside; imports list what's needed; exports list what other modules can use; providers list services; bootstrap defines the root component. Modern Angular 14+ prefers standalone components (no NgModule).

✅ ADVANTAGES

PropertyPurposeExample
declarationsComponents/directives/pipes owned by modulePortfolioCardComponent, HighlightDirective
importsOther modules this module needsCommonModule, HttpClientModule
exportsWhat other modules can accessExport components for reuse
providersServices available to modulePortfolioService, AuthService

🚀 ANGULAR EXAMPLE (TypeScript)

Feature module for portfolio management (legacy):

@NgModule({
  declarations: [PortfolioListComponent, PortfolioCardComponent],
  imports: [CommonModule, ReactiveFormsModule, PortfolioRoutingModule],
  exports: [PortfolioListComponent],
  providers: [PortfolioService]
})
export class PortfolioModule { }

// Modern standalone (Angular 14+, recommended)
@Component({
  selector: 'app-portfolio',
  standalone: true,
  imports: [CommonModule, ReactiveFormsModule]  // No NgModule!
})
export class PortfolioComponent { }

🎤 FINAL INTERVIEW ANSWER

NgModule is a container for related code. Declarations list what the module owns; imports list what modules it depends on; exports list what's visible to other modules; providers list services; bootstrap lists the root component. In legacy FolioTrack, PortfolioModule declares its components, imports CommonModule for *ngIf and *ngFor, exports components so the app can import them, and provides PortfolioService. Modern Angular 16+ uses standalone components: each component declares dependencies directly, and NgModule becomes optional. Standalone components import what they need in the @Component decorator, so no NgModule wrapping is needed. If learning Angular today, prioritize standalone — simpler and the Angular team's direction. NgModules still matter for lazy loading large feature modules and backward compatibility.

🧠 ONE LINE MEMORY TRICK

NgModule = passport office. Declarations = staff, imports = services, exports = customers, providers = resources.

followUps.question: How do you skip the interceptor for specific requests?
followUps.answer: Since all requests pass through all interceptors, you sometimes need to skip one — e.g., the auth interceptor shouldn't attach a token to the login or register endpoints (there's no token yet, and attaching a stale one might cause errors). Solutions: (1) URL check: inside the interceptor, check req.url.includes('/auth/') and call next(req) without modification. (2) HttpContext (Angular 12+): create a context token export const SKIP_AUTH = new HttpContextToken(() => false), set it on the request: this.http.post('/login', body, { context: new HttpContext().set(SKIP_AUTH, true) }), and check in the interceptor: if (req.context.get(SKIP_AUTH)) return next(req). HttpContext is cleaner because the skip logic lives at the call site rather than in the interceptor. (3) In FolioTrack, I used a URL allowlist — any request to /api/auth/ bypassed the token interceptor.

followUps.question: How do you implement a loading indicator interceptor?
followUps.answer: A loading interceptor increments a counter on each outgoing request and decrements it when the response arrives (success or error). When the counter goes above 0, a spinner shows; when it reaches 0, the spinner hides. Implementation: loadingInterceptor: HttpInterceptorFn = (req, next) => { const loading = inject(LoadingService); loading.show(); return next(req).pipe(finalize(() => loading.hide())); }. The finalize operator runs on both success and error, ensuring the spinner always disappears. For multiple simultaneous requests, use a counter in LoadingService: activeRequests++ on show, if (--activeRequests === 0) hide(). In FolioTrack, this pattern showed a top progress bar (similar to YouTube's) during any pending API calls without any per-component loading state management.

questions.tip: Interceptor order matters. Interceptors execute in registration order for requests, and in reverse order for responses. Register auth first (so the token is attached early), error handler last (so it sees all responses). In Angular 15+, use functional interceptors (HttpInterceptorFn) — simpler, no class boilerplate, and fully tree-shakeable.

questions.question: What is NgModule in Angular? Explain declarations, imports, exports, providers, and bootstrap.
questions.answer:
📌 SIMPLE DEFINITION

Constructor runs when the class is instantiated (dependency injection happens). ngOnInit runs after initialization — @Input properties are set. Use constructor for DI only; use ngOnInit for initialization logic and data fetching.

✅ ADVANTAGES

AspectConstructorngOnInit
WhenClass instantiation, before change detectionAfter @Input set, after first change detection
@Input availableNoYes
Use forDependency Injection onlyFetch data, initialize state

🚀 ANGULAR EXAMPLE (TypeScript)

Portfolio detail showing constructor vs ngOnInit timing:

@Component({
  selector: 'app-portfolio-detail',
  template: `<p>{{ portfolio?.name }}</p>`
})
export class PortfolioDetailComponent implements OnInit {
  @Input() portfolioId!: string;
  portfolio: Portfolio | null = null;

  // Constructor runs FIRST, BEFORE @Input is set
  constructor(private portfolioService: PortfolioService) {
    console.log('1. Constructor — portfolioId is', this.portfolioId);  // undefined!
  }

  // ngOnInit runs SECOND, after @Input is set
  ngOnInit(): void {
    console.log('2. ngOnInit — portfolioId is', this.portfolioId);  // set!
    this.portfolioService.getById(this.portfolioId)
      .pipe(takeUntilDestroyed())
      .subscribe(p => this.portfolio = p);
  }
}

// Output:
// 1. Constructor — portfolioId is undefined
// 2. ngOnInit — portfolioId is portfolio-123

🎤 FINAL INTERVIEW ANSWER

Constructor is called when a class is instantiated — this is where Angular dependency injection happens. Inject services and store them; avoid complex logic. ngOnInit is a lifecycle hook that runs after the component is initialized: @Input properties are set, change detection has run once. Use ngOnInit for initialization logic: fetch data, set up subscriptions, initialize state. In PortfolioDetailComponent, the constructor injects PortfolioService. ngOnInit runs after the parent sets portfolioId via @Input, so I fetch the portfolio using that ID. A common mistake is trying to access @Input in the constructor — it won't be set yet. Modern Angular with takeUntilDestroyed() eliminates manual unsubscription in ngOnDestroy. This lifecycle order prevents bugs: DI first, then initialization.

🧠 ONE LINE MEMORY TRICK

Constructor = DI only. ngOnInit = initialize and fetch. ngAfterViewInit = access DOM.

followUps.question: What is SharedModule and when do you use it?
followUps.answer: A SharedModule is a convention (not a built-in Angular concept) — a module that declares and exports reusable components, directives, and pipes used across multiple feature modules. Instead of importing CommonModule, ReactiveFormsModule, and 10 shared components in every feature module, you import SharedModule once. The SharedModule re-exports what features need: exports: [CommonModule, ReactiveFormsModule, LoadingSpinnerComponent, ...]. Rules: SharedModule should never have providers (services) — only declarations and re-exports. If you put a service in SharedModule, importing it in a lazy module creates a second instance. Services belong in root or their own feature module. In Angular 17+ standalone architecture, SharedModule is replaced by directly importing individual standalone components/directives in each component's imports array — more explicit, better tree-shaking.

followUps.question: What is the difference between lazy-loaded modules and eagerly loaded modules?
followUps.answer: Eagerly loaded modules are imported in AppModule.imports (or in app routes without loadChildren) — they are downloaded and initialized when the app first loads, increasing initial bundle size. Lazy-loaded modules use loadChildren: () => import('./feature/feature.module').then(m => m.FeatureModule) in routes — Angular splits them into separate JS chunks that are only downloaded when the user navigates to that route. Impact: an app with 10 feature modules eager-loads everything upfront (slow initial load). With lazy loading, the initial bundle contains only the shell; feature code loads on demand. Critical for large apps. Tip: use the Angular DevTools or ng build --stats-json + webpack-bundle-analyzer to visualize bundle sizes and spot modules that should be lazy-loaded.

questions.tip: In Angular 17+ with standalone components, NgModule is no longer required. You'll still need to understand NgModule for maintaining existing projects and using third-party libraries that haven't migrated. The Angular team recommends standalone components for all new code.

questions.question: What is the difference between constructor and ngOnInit in Angular?
questions.answer:
📌 SIMPLE DEFINITION

JIT (Just-In-Time) compiles in the browser at runtime. AOT (Ahead-Of-Time) compiles before deployment. AOT is smaller, faster, safer; JIT is slower but instant feedback in dev. Angular 9+ uses AOT by default.

✅ ADVANTAGES

AspectJITAOT
CompiledIn browser at runtimeBefore deployment
Bundle sizeLarger (3-4 MB)Smaller (1 MB)
StartupSlowerFaster
ErrorsRuntime (users see)Build time (you fix)

🚀 ANGULAR EXAMPLE (Commands)

Build commands for each strategy:

# AOT (default, recommended)
ng build
# Output: optimized JS, smaller, caught errors at build time

# Check build size
ng build --stats-json
webpack-bundle-analyzer dist/stats.json

🎤 FINAL INTERVIEW ANSWER

JIT compilation happens in the browser: Angular code and the compiler ship to users, compile in the browser, then run. Bundle is larger (~3-4 MB with compiler), startup is slower, but instant feedback in development. AOT compiles during build on your machine: pre-compiled optimized JavaScript ships to the browser. Bundle is smaller (~1 MB), startup is faster, and template errors are caught before deploy. Angular 9+ uses AOT by default (ng build and ng serve both use it). In FolioTrack, AOT catches type mismatches like {{ portfolio.value }} when the component doesn't have a value property, before any user sees an error. The tradeoff is longer build time (20-30 seconds vs 5 with JIT), but smaller bundle and caught errors are essential for production fintech apps.

🧠 ONE LINE MEMORY TRICK

JIT = compile in browser (big, slow). AOT = compile at build time (small, fast, errors caught early).

followUps.question: What if you need to react when an @Input() value changes after initialisation?
followUps.answer: Use ngOnChanges — it fires every time an @Input() property changes, including the first time (before ngOnInit). Receives a SimpleChanges object with current and previous values: ngOnChanges(changes: SimpleChanges): void { if (changes['portfolioId'] && !changes['portfolioId'].firstChange) { this.loadPortfolio(changes['portfolioId'].currentValue); } }. With Angular Signals (v17+), use an input() signal and an effect() instead — the effect re-runs automatically whenever the input signal's value changes: portfolioId = input.required<number>(); constructor() { effect(() => this.loadPortfolio(this.portfolioId())); }. This is cleaner than ngOnChanges, especially for multiple dependent inputs.

followUps.question: Can you call async operations in the constructor?
followUps.answer: Technically yes, but you should not. Constructors are expected to be synchronous, fast, and side-effect-free. Angular's DI system calls the constructor; if it throws or does unexpected async work, it can break DI. HTTP calls in the constructor also run before @Input values are set, so any logic that depends on inputs will break silently. Testing is also harder — unit tests that create the component will trigger HTTP calls in the constructor even when you don't want them. The correct pattern: DI in constructor, everything else in ngOnInit. If you need data to be available as early as possible (before navigation completes), use a Route Resolver instead — it pre-fetches data before the component is instantiated at all.

questions.tip: Simple rule: Constructor = inject dependencies only. ngOnInit = everything else (HTTP calls, form setup, subscriptions, logic that uses @Input values). If an interviewer asks why not use the constructor for HTTP calls, the answer is: @Input values aren't set yet, testing is harder, and constructors should be side-effect-free.

questions.question: What is the difference between JIT and AOT compilation in Angular?
questions.answer:
📌 SIMPLE DEFINITION

Dependency Injection is giving objects their dependencies from outside instead of creating them. @Injectable marks a service as injectable. Angular's DI container manages creation and injection — components request services, Angular provides them.

✅ ADVANTAGES

AdvantageWhy It Matters
Loose couplingComponent depends on interface, not implementation
TestablePass mock service; no HTTP calls in tests
FlexibleProvide different impl for dev vs production
Singleton managementAll components share one service instance

🚀 ANGULAR EXAMPLE (TypeScript & Test)

Service with DI and unit testing:

@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })
export class PortfolioService {
  constructor(private http: HttpClient) {}
  getPortfolios(): Observable<Portfolio[]> {
    return this.http.get<Portfolio[]>('/api/portfolios');
  }
}

@Component({
  selector: 'app-portfolio',
  template: `<p *ngFor="let p of portfolios">{{ p.name }}</p>`
})
export class PortfolioComponent implements OnInit {
  portfolios: Portfolio[] = [];
  constructor(private portfolioService: PortfolioService) {}  // DI

  ngOnInit(): void {
    this.portfolioService.getPortfolios()
      .subscribe(p => this.portfolios = p);
  }
}

// Unit test with mock
class MockPortfolioService {
  getPortfolios(): Observable<Portfolio[]> {
    return of([{ id: '1', name: 'Mock' }]);
  }
}

TestBed.configureTestingModule({
  providers: [
    { provide: PortfolioService, useClass: MockPortfolioService }
  ]
});

🎤 FINAL INTERVIEW ANSWER

Dependency Injection decouples code by injecting dependencies instead of creating them. @Injectable marks a service as injectable; providedIn: 'root' makes it a singleton across the app. PortfolioComponent declares PortfolioService in its constructor, and Angular provides it. This decoupling enables testing: I inject a mock service in tests, unit test logic without HTTP calls. For production, the real service is injected. DI is Angular's core pattern: every component and service is injectable and composable. In FolioTrack, I inject PortfolioService, AuthService, HttpInterceptors, and custom validators — DI wires them all together. Without DI, testing and swapping implementations becomes tedious and tightly coupled.

🧠 ONE LINE MEMORY TRICK

DI = dependencies injected, not created. @Injectable = can be injected. constructor(service) = I need this.

followUps.question: What is Ivy and how does it relate to AOT?
followUps.answer: Ivy is Angular's current compilation and rendering engine (introduced in Angular 9, became the only engine in Angular 12). Before Ivy, Angular had the View Engine. Ivy improved AOT significantly: AOT compilation is now the default even in development (ng serve uses AOT), which means template errors are caught immediately in the terminal rather than at runtime. Ivy also generates smaller bundles (better tree-shaking at the instruction level), faster compilation (incremental compilation), and improved debugging (component instances visible in browser DevTools via ng.getContext($0)). Ivy's localisation approach also allows lazy compilation of individual components, enabling the standalone components architecture introduced in Angular 14.

followUps.question: What is tree-shaking and how does AOT enable it?
followUps.answer: Tree-shaking is the build tool's process of removing dead code — code that is imported but never actually used. AOT enables Angular-specific tree-shaking in two ways: (1) The Angular compiler is not included in production bundles (it's needed for JIT but not AOT). (2) Components, directives, and pipes that are never used are excluded from the final bundle — but only if they're in standalone components or properly exported. With traditional NgModules, everything declared in a module is bundled even if only one component uses it. Standalone components are individually tree-shakeable. Practical impact: FolioTrack (Angular 19, standalone) had an initial bundle of ~120KB. The same app with all features eagerly loaded in one NgModule would have been 3-4x larger. AOT + tree-shaking + lazy loading together are the key to small Angular bundle sizes.

questions.tip: From Angular 9 (Ivy), AOT is the default in BOTH development and production. You don't need to choose anymore. The key interview point: AOT catches template errors at build time (not runtime), reduces bundle size (compiler not shipped), and enables better tree-shaking. JIT is essentially a historical concept now.

questions.question: How does Dependency Injection work in Angular? What is @Injectable?
questions.answer:
📌 SIMPLE DEFINITION

Angular routing maps URLs to components. Lazy loading defers loading feature modules until the user navigates to them — faster initial load. Think of a shopping app: load the home module immediately, load the checkout module only when the user starts checking out.

✅ ADVANTAGES

FeatureBenefit
Basic routingMap /portfolio to PortfolioComponent
Lazy loadingLoad AdminModule only on /admin — faster initial load
Route guardscanActivate guards prevent unauthorized access
Nested routesFeature modules have their own router-outlet

🚀 ANGULAR EXAMPLE (TypeScript & Routing)

App routing with lazy loading:

// app-routing.module.ts
const routes: Routes = [
  { path: '', component: HomeComponent },
  { path: 'portfolio', component: PortfolioComponent },
  // Lazy load AdminModule only when /admin is accessed
  { path: 'admin', loadChildren: () => import('./admin/admin.module').then(m => m.AdminModule) },
  { path: '**', redirectTo: '' }
];

@NgModule({
  imports: [RouterModule.forRoot(routes)],
  exports: [RouterModule]
})
export class AppRoutingModule { }

// app.component.html
<nav>
  <a routerLink="/">Home</a>
  <a routerLink="/portfolio">Portfolio</a>
  <a routerLink="/admin">Admin</a>  <!-- Loads only when clicked -->
</nav>
<router-outlet></router-outlet>

🎤 FINAL INTERVIEW ANSWER

Angular Router maps URLs to components. Basic routing: { path: 'portfolio', component: PortfolioComponent } loads the component when the user navigates to /portfolio. Lazy loading defers loading entire feature modules until needed: { path: 'admin', loadChildren: () => import('./admin/admin.module') } loads AdminModule only when the user visits /admin — this improves initial page load. Route guards like canActivate prevent unauthorized access: a guard can check authentication and redirect to login if needed. In FolioTrack, the app loads the home and portfolio modules immediately, but lazy-loads the AdminModule and ReportsModule only when accessed. Nested routes let feature modules have their own router-outlet and child routes. ActivatedRoute lets components access route params and query strings. Router.navigate() programmatically navigates. This architecture scales large apps: load critical features first, defer advanced features until needed.

🧠 ONE LINE MEMORY TRICK

Routing = URLs to components. Lazy load = load module only when user navigates to it (faster initial load).

followUps.question: What is the injector hierarchy in Angular?
followUps.answer: Angular has a hierarchical injector tree that mirrors the component tree. Injectors: Root injector — app-wide singleton, created at bootstrap. Provides services with providedIn: 'root'. Module injectors — one per NgModule. Services in a module's providers array are scoped here. Lazy-loaded modules get their own injector (which is why providing a service in AppModule vs FeatureModule matters). Element injectors — one per component with a providers array. Child components get their own instance, isolated from the rest of the app. When Angular resolves a dependency, it walks up the injector hierarchy until it finds a provider — or throws a NullInjectorError. This hierarchy is why you can "shadow" a root service in a specific subtree: provide a different implementation at component level and all children in that subtree get the overridden version.

followUps.question: What is inject() function in Angular 14+?
followUps.answer: The inject() function (Angular 14+) is an alternative to constructor injection that can be used in any injection context — constructors, field initialisers, factory functions, and functional guards/interceptors. const service = inject(MyService) is equivalent to declaring private service: MyService in the constructor. Benefits: (1) Can be called in field initialisers — private service = inject(MyService) — keeping related code together. (2) Enables composable injection utilities (helper functions that call inject() inside): function injectPortfolioId() { return inject(ActivatedRoute).snapshot.params['id']; }. (3) Required for functional guards and interceptors which don't have constructors. (4) Works in derived classes without needing to pass services through super(). In FolioTrack (Angular 19), I used inject() throughout — cleaner than long constructor parameter lists.

questions.tip: Angular 14+ inject() function is the modern alternative to constructor injection. It's required for functional guards/interceptors and very useful in standalone components. takeUntilDestroyed() also uses inject(DestroyRef) internally — that's why it must be called in an injection context (constructor or field initialiser).

questions.question: How does Angular routing work and what is lazy loading?
questions.answer:
📌 SIMPLE DEFINITION

Data binding synchronizes the component TypeScript class and HTML template — changes in one reflect in the other automatically. Angular offers four types: interpolation {{ }}, property binding [ ], event binding ( ), and two-way binding [( )].

✅ ADVANTAGES

TypeSyntaxDirection
Interpolation{{ value }}Component → Template (display)
Property[disabled]="isLoading"Component → Template (set props)
Event(click)="buy()"Template → Component (events)
Two-way[(ngModel)]="query"Both directions

🚀 ANGULAR EXAMPLE (TypeScript & Template)

Stock trader using all four binding types:

@Component({
  template: `
    <!-- Interpolation -->
    <h2>Portfolio: ₹{{ balance }}</h2>
    
    <!-- Property binding -->
    <button [disabled]="isLoading">Buy</button>
    
    <!-- Event binding -->
    <button (click)="sellStock()">Sell</button>
    
    <!-- Two-way binding -->
    <input [(ngModel)]="searchQuery" placeholder="Search...">
    <p>Searching: {{ searchQuery }}</p>
  `
})
export class StockTraderComponent {
  balance = 500000;
  isLoading = false;
  searchQuery = '';

  sellStock(): void {
    this.isLoading = true;
    setTimeout(() => this.isLoading = false, 2000);
  }
}

🎤 FINAL INTERVIEW ANSWER

Data binding is the automatic synchronization between component class and template. Interpolation {{ balance }} displays values. Property binding [disabled]="isLoading" sets element properties or component inputs. Event binding (click)="sellStock()" calls component methods on user actions. Two-way binding [(ngModel)]="searchQuery" syncs both directions — type in the input and searchQuery updates; change searchQuery in code and the input updates. Angular watches for changes in the component and updates the DOM. It watches the DOM for events and updates the component. This declarative approach eliminates manual DOM manipulation — I focus on data, Angular handles rendering. In a trading dashboard, I use interpolation to show prices, property binding to disable buttons during loading, event binding to handle buy/sell clicks, and two-way binding on search inputs.

🧠 ONE LINE MEMORY TRICK

{{ }} = show. [prop] = set. (event) = react. [(both)] = both sync.

followUps.question: What are route guards (CanActivate, CanDeactivate)?
followUps.answer: Route guards control navigation. canActivate — runs before navigating to a route; returning false blocks navigation. Use for authentication checks: if no token → redirect to login. canDeactivate — runs before leaving a route; returning false keeps the user on the page. Use for unsaved-changes warnings: "You have unsaved changes. Leave anyway?". canLoad (deprecated in Angular 15+, use canMatch) — blocks the lazy-loaded module from being downloaded at all, not just navigation. In Angular 14+, use functional guards (plain functions returning boolean | UrlTree | Observable<...>) instead of class-based guards. Example: export const authGuard = () => inject(AuthService).isLoggedIn() ? true : inject(Router).createUrlTree(['/login']);

followUps.question: What is the Router state?
followUps.answer: The Router state is a tree of ActivatedRoute objects representing the current URL structure. Each route segment has an ActivatedRoute with: params (path params like :id), queryParams (query string), data (static data from route config), snapshot (synchronous current value), and Observables that emit on changes. Access in a component: constructor(private route: ActivatedRoute) {}. Read param: this.route.params.subscribe(p => this.id = +p['id']) (Observable, updates on navigation). For one-time reads: this.route.snapshot.params['id']. The RouterStateSnapshot in guards gives the full URL tree being navigated to, useful for redirect-after-login (save the target URL, redirect after login succeeds).

questions.tip: Route Guards: canActivate = prevent navigation to a route (auth check). canDeactivate = prevent leaving a route ("You have unsaved changes"). In Angular 15+ use functional guards (plain functions) instead of class-based guards.
